source_metadata.title_raw: Caren Ray - A-18-176 - October 4, 2018 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 4, 2018

Caren Ray
Arroyo Grande City Council
756 Myrtle Street
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-176

Dear Ms. Ray:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, the Mayor Pro Tem of the City of Arroyo Grande, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the Brisco Road Interchange Project given that your residence is located within approximately 4,600 feet of the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. Highway 101 ("U.S. 101") which would be improved under two of three design options for the Project under consideration?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your residence based on the facts presented.

FACTS

The Brisco Road Interchange Project began in the late 1990s. The purpose of the Project is "to investigate, identify, and propose potential modifications to the [northbound U.S. 101] on and

_______________________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-176
Page No. 2

off-ramps at Brisco Road in order to alleviate traffic congestion at peak travel times." Among other things, the Project will determine the future location of the northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps currently located at the intersection of Brisco Road and U.S. 101. The Project is a joint venture between the City of Arroyo Grande and the California Department of Transportation ("Caltrans"). The City is responsible for the Project's design, technical documents and environmental review, and Caltrans is the Project's lead agency under the federal National Environmental Policy Act.

The City Council will soon decide between the following three design options for the Project:

• Option 1: The City would close the existing northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at Brisco Road, complete improvements at the intersections of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101 and Grand Avenue and U.S. 101, and complete the widening of the U.S. 101 overpass at Grand Avenue. The estimated cost of this option is $14,000,000.

• Option 2: The City would relocate the northbound U.S. 101 on and off-ramps at Brisco Road to Rodeo Drive, complete improvements at the intersections of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane and Grand Avenue and U.S. 101. The estimated cost of this option is $23,000,000.

• Option 3: There would be "no build," and there is no cost indicated for this option.

The City Council will also consider adopting findings necessary for the approval of a Mitigated Negative Declaration for the Project, select a design alternative for the Project, and provide direction to staff on how to budget for the selected design alternative.

Your residence is a single-family home located within a residential neighborhood in the portion of the City northeast of the junction of State Route 227 ("S.R. 227") and U.S. 101. Your residence is approximately:

• 1.9 miles from the intersection of Camino Mercado and U.S. 101;

• 1.4 miles from the intersection of U.S. 101 and Brisco Road;

• 1.3 miles from the intersection of U.S. 101 and Rodeo Drive;

• 1.1 miles from the intersection of Rodeo Drive and Grace Lane; and

• 4,600 feet from the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision if that decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. Of the interests, the facts presented indicate that you have a real

File No. A-18-176
Page No. 3

property interest in your residence, assuming you have an interest in that real property worth $2,000
or more, and an interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members, with
respect to decisions relating to the Brisco Road Interchange Project.

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest
that is explicitly involved in the decision at issue. This regulation provides that a decision's effect
on an official's interest is presumed reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision. Under this regulation, an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the
decision involves any governmental decision affecting a real property financial interest as described
in Regulation 18702.2(a)(1)-(6).

Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on
an official's real property interest. Paragraph (6) of subdivision (a) of that regulation provides that a
decision's effect on an official's real property interest is material if the decision involves
construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and
the official will receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of the decision.

The decisions relating to the Project involve the construction of, and improvements to, U.S.
101 on and off-ramps and street intersections that are various distances from your residence. On and
off-ramps are adjacent to streets, and street intersections involve streets by definition. Therefore, at
issue is whether you will receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of any of those
decisions.

This is the third in a series of recent advice letters relating to the Project. In the Hill Advice
Letter, No. A-18-092, we concluded that the Act prohibits the Mayor of Arroyo Grande from taking
part in decisions relating to the selection of a design option for the Project because those decisions
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest in his
residence. Applying Regulation 18702.2(a)(6), we determined that the Mayor would receive
disproportionate benefits and detriments as a result of those decisions, including substantially
increased or decreased traffic immediately southwest of his residence, enhanced or diminished
access to northbound U.S. 101, and an improved intersection within 920 feet of his residence,
depending on which design option the City selects for the Project.

In the Harmon Advice Letter, No. A-18-172, however, we concluded that the Act would not
prohibit the Arroyo Grande City Councilmember at issue from taking part in decisions relating to
the Project because those decisions would not have a disqualifying effect on her real property
interest in residence. Again, applying Regulation 18702.2(a)(6), we determined that the
Councilmember would not receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of those
decisions. The Councilmember's residence is located in the portion of the City northeast of the
junction of S.R. 227 and U.S. 101, significantly farther from all of the Project's potential
intersection improvements than the Mayor's residence. Although the Councilmember's residence is
located only approximately 2,100 feet from the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101, any
benefit or detriment she would receive from the Project's potential improvement of that intersection
would not be disproportionate compared to that received by other residents of the portion of the
City northeast of the junction of S.R. 227 and U.S. 101.

File No. A-18-176
Page No. 4

We now examine whether you would receive a disproportionate benefit or detriment as a result of decisions relating to the Project. Your residence is further northeast of the junction of S.R. 227 and U.S. 101, and thus more attenuated from all of the Project's potential intersection improvements, than the Councilmember's residence at issue in the Harmon Advice Letter. Moreover, the facts presented provide no indication that any decisions relating to the Project would result in your receipt of a disproportionate benefit or detriment.

The Project's only potential intersection improvement within a mile of your residence is the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 101, located approximately 4,600 feet from your residence. That potential intersection improvement may result in your receipt of the benefits of upgraded aesthetics and improved traffic flow nearby your residence, and the detriments of temporary construction and related traffic impacts. However, like the Councilmember at issue in the Harmon Advice Letter, any benefit or detriment you receive as a result of that potential intersection improvement would not be disproportionate compared to that received by other residents of the portion of the City northeast of the junction of S.R. 227 and U.S. 101.

Therefore, based on the facts presented, the decisions relating to the Project would not result in your receipt of a disproportionate benefit or detriment under Regulation 18702.2(a)(6). Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your residence.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

David M. Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

residence is located within approximately 4,600 feet of the intersection of Grand Avenue and U.S. 
Highway 101 ("U.S. 101 ") which would be improved under two of three design options for the 
Project under consideration?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in those decisions because they would 
not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your real property interest in your 
residence based on the facts presented.
